items.subject: social_psychology
items.transcript: Stereotype threat is one of the more unsettling findings in social psychology, because it shows how performance can be shaped by something as subtle as a reminder. The term refers to the anxiety people feel when they're in a situation where they could confirm a negative stereotype about a group they belong to. And here's the key point: that anxiety itself can drag down their performance, even when the stereotype has nothing to do with their actual ability. Let me give you the foundational study. In the nineteen-nineties, Claude Steele and Joshua Aronson gave a difficult verbal test to Black and white college students. For one group, they described it simply as a problem-solving exercise. For another group, they called it a genuine measure of intellectual ability. Now, when it was just a problem-solving exercise, both groups performed about the same. But when it was framed as a real test of intelligence, the Black students scored significantly lower, even though the two groups were equally prepared. The framing alone activated the threat. What's striking is that the students weren't consciously worried about confirming a stereotype. The anxiety operated below awareness, consuming mental resources that should have gone toward the test itself. Your working memory, in other words, gets partly occupied by the worry. This matters enormously for how we interpret achievement gaps. A lower score doesn't necessarily reflect lower ability; it may reflect the conditions under which the test was taken. And it suggests something hopeful, too. Simple interventions, like reframing a task or affirming a student's values beforehand, can reduce the threat and narrow the gap.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How a subtle psychological threat can lower test performance
options.B: How Black and white students differ in verbal ability
options.C: The history of intelligence testing in American colleges
options.D: Why working memory declines as people grow older

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。整堂课的核心是"刻板印象威胁"——一种微妙的心理威胁如何在能力相同的情况下拉低考试成绩，这正是教授反复强调的主旨。B错误（wrong_topic），课程明确指出两组能力相同，重点不是群体间真实能力差异，而是威胁带来的影响。C错误（too_narrow），智力测验只是研究的背景，不是课程主题，且课程并未讲测验史。D错误（surface_word_trap），课程提到了"working memory"这个词，但只是解释机制，与衰老完全无关。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happened when the test was described as a problem-solving exercise?

options.A: Black students scored significantly higher than white students
options.B: Both groups of students performed at about the same level
options.C: Both groups performed worse than they had expected to
options.D: White students became more anxious than Black students

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授明确说"when it was just a problem-solving exercise, both groups performed about the same"，即两组表现相当。A错误（reversed_logic），课程说在被称为智力测验时黑人学生分数显著更低，而非更高，方向相反。C错误（topic_adjacent），课程从未提到两组都低于预期。D错误（wrong_detail），课程说被框定为智力测验时黑人学生表现下降，并未说白人学生更焦虑，篡改了关键事实。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why did the anxiety lower the students' scores?

options.A: Because the students had not prepared adequately for the test
options.B: Because the test questions were deliberately made unfair
options.C: Because the worry consumed mental resources needed for the test
options.D: Because the students consciously chose to give up early

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授解释焦虑"consuming mental resources that should have gone toward the test itself"，并说工作记忆被担忧部分占用。A错误（wrong_detail），课程明确说两组"equally prepared"，准备程度不是原因。B错误（topic_adjacent），课程从未说题目不公平。D错误（reversed_logic），课程强调焦虑在意识之下运作，学生并非有意放弃，与"consciously chose to give up"相反。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about achievement gaps between groups?

options.A: They are entirely caused by differences in natural ability
options.B: They can never be reduced once they have appeared
options.C: They prove that intelligence tests are completely useless
options.D: They may partly reflect testing conditions rather than ability

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授说"A lower score doesn't necessarily reflect lower ability; it may reflect the conditions under which the test was taken"，暗示成绩差距可能部分源于测试条件而非能力。A错误（reversed_logic），课程恰恰反对差距完全由先天能力造成。B错误（topic_adjacent），课程结尾说简单干预能缩小差距，暗示差距可被减少，与"never be reduced"相反。C错误（over_generalization），课程指出测验受条件影响，但并未推断测验"completely useless"，言过其实。

items.subject: psychology
items.topic: the mere exposure effect in advertising
items.transcript: Have you ever found yourself liking a song you didn't care for at first, simply because you kept hearing it on the radio? That feeling has a name in psychology: the mere exposure effect. The idea is straightforward. The more often we're exposed to something, the more we tend to like it, even if we never consciously think about it. Familiarity, by itself, breeds preference. The effect was studied systematically by Robert Zajonc in the nineteen-sixties. In one experiment, he showed people a series of made-up symbols and nonsense words, some many times and others just once. Afterward, he asked which ones they preferred. People reliably rated the items they'd seen most often as more pleasant, even though they couldn't remember having seen them before. The recognition wasn't conscious, but the preference was real. Now, this is exactly why advertisers repeat the same brand name and jingle over and over. They aren't trying to win a logical argument about why their product is best. They're simply building familiarity, so that when you stand in the store facing a shelf of similar options, one name feels warmer and more trustworthy than the rest. You don't need to remember the ad; you just need to feel that small flicker of recognition. Of course, there's a limit. Repeat something too many times and people grow bored or even irritated, which advertisers call wear-out. But within that range, sheer repetition is a powerful and surprisingly cheap tool. It works precisely because it slips past our defenses, shaping preference without ever asking for our agreement.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How advertisers design memorable jingles and slogans
options.B: How repeated exposure to something increases our liking for it
options.C: Why people forget most of the advertisements they see
options.D: How nonsense words are used to test human memory

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。整堂课围绕"mere exposure effect"——反复接触某事物会增加我们的喜爱，这是反复强调的主旨。A错误（too_narrow），广告口号只是一个应用例子，不是课程主题。C错误（wrong_topic），课程说人们记不住广告，但主题是喜好的形成，而非遗忘本身。D错误（surface_word_trap），"nonsense words"出现在实验里，但只是手段，课程不是讲记忆测试。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did people do in Zajonc's experiment?

options.A: They correctly remembered which symbols they had seen before
options.B: They preferred symbols that were paired with pleasant music
options.C: They rated frequently seen items as more pleasant than rare ones
options.D: They disliked the symbols they had been shown most often

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授说人们"reliably rated the items they'd seen most often as more pleasant"。A错误（reversed_logic），课程明确说人们"couldn't remember having seen them before"，并非准确记得。B错误（topic_adjacent），实验中并未配对音乐，是无关细节。D错误（reversed_logic），看得越多越喜欢，而非更讨厌，方向颠倒。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention advertisers repeating a brand name and jingle?

options.A: To criticize advertising for misleading consumers
options.B: To prove that jingles are more effective than logos
options.C: To describe how advertisers write their slogans
options.D: To illustrate a real-world use of the mere exposure effect

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授提到广告反复重复品牌名，是为了举例说明"mere exposure effect"在现实中的应用——通过熟悉感建立偏好。A错误（wrong_purpose），教授并非在批评广告误导，而是解释机制。B错误（over_inference），课程从未比较jingle和logo的效果。C错误（literal_restatement），这只是复述"广告重复"这一字面动作，没有解释"为什么"教授要提它。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the mere exposure effect?

options.A: It can shape our preferences without our conscious awareness
options.B: It works equally well no matter how often something is repeated
options.C: It only influences people who pay close attention to ads
options.D: It has been completely replaced by newer advertising methods

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授说该效应"slips past our defenses, shaping preference without ever asking for our agreement"，且偏好在无意识中形成，暗示它能在我们无察觉时塑造喜好。B错误（reversed_logic），课程提到"wear-out"，重复过多会令人厌烦，并非任何次数都同样有效。C错误（reversed_logic），效应恰恰对不刻意注意的人也起作用，无需集中注意。D错误（topic_adjacent），课程称其为"powerful and surprisingly cheap tool"，从未说它被取代。

items.subject: architecture
items.topic: how Roman concrete allowed the building of large domes
items.transcript: When you walk into the Pantheon in Rome and look up at that enormous dome, it's hard to believe it was built almost two thousand years ago. For centuries it remained the largest dome in the world. So how did the Romans do it? The answer is a material we call Roman concrete. The Romans made their concrete by mixing volcanic ash, lime, and water, and then adding small pieces of rock or rubble as filler. What made this mixture special was the volcanic ash. It reacted chemically with the lime and water to form a very strong, stable bond, one that could even set underwater. This gave Roman concrete a durability that ordinary stone construction couldn't match. But the really clever part is how they used it to build domes. A solid stone dome that large would have been impossibly heavy and might have collapsed under its own weight. So the Roman builders did something smart. As the dome rose higher toward the top, they mixed in lighter materials, like volcanic pumice, instead of heavy rock. The lower part of the dome is made with denser, heavier aggregate, while the upper part is much lighter. This gradual change in weight allowed the dome to support itself without crumbling. They also left a large opening at the very top, which reduced the weight even further. The result was a structure both lighter and stronger than anyone before them had managed. And it has lasted, remarkably, right up to the present day.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The religious purpose of the Pantheon in ancient Rome
options.B: How volcanic eruptions shaped early Roman cities
options.C: How Roman concrete made it possible to build large domes
options.D: Why modern concrete is weaker than ancient concrete

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。整堂课讲罗马混凝土的成分以及它如何使大型穹顶得以建造，这是主旨。A错误（wrong_topic），课程从未谈万神殿的宗教用途。B错误（surface_word_trap），"volcanic"一词出现多次，但课程讲的是火山灰作建材，不是火山喷发塑造城市。D错误（too_narrow），课程虽提到罗马混凝土很耐久，但并未拿现代混凝土作比较，这不是主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what made Roman concrete especially strong?

options.A: The large pieces of rubble used as filler
options.B: The high temperatures at which it was baked
options.C: The iron rods placed inside the mixture
options.D: The volcanic ash that reacted with lime and water

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授说"What made this mixture special was the volcanic ash. It reacted chemically with the lime and water to form a very strong, stable bond"。A错误（wrong_detail），碎石只是填料，课程说让它特殊的是火山灰，而非碎石。B错误（topic_adjacent），课程从未提到高温烘烤。C错误（topic_adjacent），课程没有提到铁棒，这是现代钢筋混凝土的概念，非本课内容。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention volcanic pumice?

options.A: To explain how builders made the top of the dome lighter
options.B: To describe where the Romans obtained their building materials
options.C: To compare Roman concrete with modern building methods
options.D: To show that the dome was decorated with volcanic stone

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授提到浮石是为了说明建造者在穹顶上部混入更轻的材料来减重，使穹顶能自我支撑。B错误（wrong_purpose），课程并非在讲材料来源地。C错误（over_inference），此处没有与现代方法作比较。D错误（literal_restatement），把浮石当装饰是对内容的错误复述，课程说浮石是为减重，不是装饰。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the opening at the top of the dome?

options.A: It was added to let rainwater drain out of the building
options.B: It helped the dome stand by reducing its overall weight
options.C: It was a mistake that later builders had to repair
options.D: It made the dome far weaker than a solid stone one

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授说"They also left a large opening at the very top, which reduced the weight even further"，紧接减重使穹顶能自撑的论述，暗示开口帮助穹顶站立。A错误（topic_adjacent），课程从未提排水。C错误（topic_adjacent），课程把开口说成聪明之举，并非需要修补的错误。D错误（reversed_logic），开口是为减重助力结构，而非让穹顶更弱，方向相反。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: the rediscovery of perspective in early Renaissance painting
items.transcript: If you compare a medieval painting with one from the early Renaissance, one difference jumps out immediately. The medieval scene looks flat, almost like a pattern, while the Renaissance scene seems to open up into real, three-dimensional space. What changed was the rediscovery of linear perspective. Linear perspective is a technique for representing depth on a flat surface. The core idea is that parallel lines appear to converge as they recede from us, meeting at a single point on the horizon called the vanishing point. Objects also shrink in a regular, mathematical way as they move farther into the distance. The key figure here is the architect Filippo Brunelleschi. Around fourteen-fifteen, he ran a famous demonstration in Florence. He painted a building, the Baptistery, on a small panel using precise perspective, and drilled a tiny hole in it. A viewer would look through the hole from behind, holding a mirror in front. When the mirror was lifted, the painted image lined up so perfectly with the real building that the two were almost indistinguishable. It was a stunning proof that painting could capture space mathematically. Now, why does this matter so much? Before perspective, painters arranged figures by importance, making a saint larger than ordinary people regardless of where they stood. Perspective replaced that symbolic logic with a unified, measurable space, the same space the viewer occupies. The painting became a window onto a coherent world. This shift didn't just change how pictures looked. It reflected a new confidence that the world could be observed, measured, and rationally understood.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The life and architectural career of Filippo Brunelleschi
options.B: Why medieval painters preferred flat, patterned images
options.C: How mirrors were used as tools in Renaissance art
options.D: How linear perspective transformed Renaissance painting

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。整堂课讲线性透视的重新发现及它如何改变文艺复兴绘画，这是主旨。A错误（too_narrow），布鲁内莱斯基是关键人物，但课程不是他的生平传记。B错误（wrong_topic），中世纪绘画的扁平只是开头对比，不是主题。C错误（surface_word_trap），镜子出现在演示里，但只是手段，课程不是讲镜子作为艺术工具。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe Brunelleschi's demonstration with the mirror?

options.A: To show that perspective could capture space with striking accuracy
options.B: To explain how mirrors were manufactured in fifteenth-century Florence
options.C: To argue that Brunelleschi was a better painter than architect
options.D: To describe what the Baptistery building looked like in detail

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授描述镜子演示是为了说明透视能以惊人的精确度再现空间——画作与真实建筑几乎无法区分，正是"painting could capture space mathematically"的证明。B错误（wrong_purpose），课程并非讲镜子制造工艺。C错误（over_inference），课程从未比较他作为画家与建筑师的高下。D错误（literal_restatement），单纯描述洗礼堂外观是对内容的复述，未说明教授提此演示的"目的"。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how did medieval painters decide the size of figures?

options.A: By how far each figure stood from the vanishing point
options.B: By the importance of each figure in the scene
options.C: By measuring distances with mathematical precision
options.D: By copying the proportions of real buildings nearby

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授说透视之前"painters arranged figures by importance, making a saint larger than ordinary people regardless of where they stood"，即按重要性决定大小。A错误（reversed_logic），按消失点距离来定大小恰恰是透视的做法，与中世纪相反。C错误（wrong_detail），数学化测量是透视引入的，不是中世纪画家的方法。D错误（topic_adjacent），课程从未说中世纪画家按附近建筑比例作画。

questions.stem: Why does the professor say the painting became 'a window onto a coherent world'?

options.A: To suggest that Renaissance paintings were displayed near windows
options.B: To explain how painters physically built frames for their works
options.C: To emphasize that perspective created a unified, measurable space
options.D: To claim that medieval art was entirely without artistic value

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授用"window onto a coherent world"这个比喻来强调透视创造了一个统一、可测量、与观者共享的空间。A错误（wrong_purpose），这是字面误读，与画作摆放位置无关。B错误（over_inference），课程从未讲制作画框。D错误（over_inference），课程对比了符号逻辑与透视，但绝未断言中世纪艺术毫无价值。
